refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating mission context into performance targets. This remains solution-neutral and avoids prescribing 'method,' 'document,' or 'history' which are design choices.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints from stakeholders), the system produces a traceable set of performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy those inputs. Verify traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall capture worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements (e.g., cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from captured worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to source mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a parameterized mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational inputs (safety plan constraints, mission objectives, environmental conditions) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters (hazard classification, operational tempo, personnel density, equipment constraints) into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix, with traceability links to source inputs.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a hierarchical baseline management system that stores, compares, and archives performance requirement snapshots tied to worksite context versions, enabling drift detection and calibration history reconstruction.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Provide a federated data model where worksite mission context (safety plan, policies, operational constraints) is maintained as a separate, versioned artifact that feeds a performance derivation workflow, with explicit dependency tracking.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined): Implement a rule-based inference engine that translates worksite mission parameters into quantified performance targets via a documented decision matrix with traceability, coupled with a hierarchical baseline management system that archives performance requirement snapshots and maintains calibration history. This pairing ensures both derivation logic transparency and historical auditability.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between mission-context data model and inference rule syntax—mitigated by defining a schema-agnostic rule language and versioned context API.
Baseline versioning tied to both context and derived targets—mitigated by explicit dependency graph in baseline metadata.
Inference engine and baseline system must share a common performance target representation—mitigated by defining a canonical performance requirement schema upfront.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP pair avoids vendor lock-in and technology prescription. The rule-based approach allows worksite policies to be updated without code changes. Baseline management is decoupled from inference logic via a clear interface (performance target schema). Traceability is maintained through explicit links in metadata, not implicit code dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies) is available in machine-readable or semi-structured form; if only unstructured documents exist, manual extraction overhead may be high.
ASSUMPTION: Performance targets can be expressed as quantified parameters (e.g., response time, accuracy, coverage); if requirements are purely qualitative, rule-based derivation becomes subjective.
RISK: Rule maintenance burden—as worksite policies evolve, decision matrix rules must be updated and re-validated; lack of governance may lead to stale or conflicting rules.
RISK: Baseline drift—if calibration updates are not systematically reviewed, historical baselines may become inconsistent with current mission context, breaking traceability.
RISK: Inference transparency—complex rule sets may become opaque; stakeholders may not understand why a particular performance target was derived, undermining acceptance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-NGHE-005 to explicitly require: (1) a documented decision matrix (or rule set) that maps worksite mission parameters to performance targets, with rationale for each mapping; (2) a baseline versioning scheme that links each performance requirement snapshot to the worksite context version and inference rule version that produced it; (3) a traceability report that shows the derivation path from worksite input → rule application → performance target for each requirement. This will clarify the DP boundary and make acceptance criteria measurable. Additionally, consider adding a sub-requirement for rule validation: 'The system shall support periodic review and sign-off of inference rules by worksite safety and operations stakeholders to ensure continued alignment with mission context.'

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Mission planning and performance baseline management subsystem that houses the rule-based inference engine, decision matrix, and hierarchical baseline archive with full traceability and calibration history

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite mission parameters (objectives, constraints, environmental conditions) from mission planning or operator input

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs quantified performance targets and baseline snapshots to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution monitoring and comparison

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: receives safety constraints that influence performance target derivation; outputs safety-critical performance thresholds

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: rule-based inference, decision matrix logic, and baseline archive are core mission-planning functions. Inference engine and historical baseline management are naturally co-located in a planning/analysis subsystem. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces outbound via Telemetry and inbound via Tasking/SafetyAlert; no algorithmic leakage into Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical execution platform, not a planning/analysis layer. Placing inference engine and baseline archive here violates separation of concerns. Would require Vehicle to host mission logic, creating tight coupling between hardware and decision logic. High diffusion: Teleoperation and Safety Services would need direct access to inference state.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is an operator interface and control layer, not a planning/analysis layer. Placing inference engine here conflates operator commands with mission-level derivation logic. Baseline archive would be orphaned from mission context. High diffusion: Vehicle and Safety Services would need to query inference state; decision matrix would be inaccessible to mission planning.

Moderate-weak fit: Safety Services may consume performance targets and safety thresholds, but should not own the inference engine or baseline archive. These are mission-level concerns, not safety-specific. Placing them here creates a safety-centric bias that obscures mission objectives. Diffusion: mission planning and Vehicle execution would need to reach into Safety Services for non-safety performance logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The rule-based inference engine and hierarchical baseline management system are inherently mission-planning and performance-analysis functions. They belong in a dedicated planning/analysis subsystem (NGHE Solution) that receives mission parameters via Tasking, applies decision logic to derive quantified targets, archives baseline snapshots, and outputs targets via Telemetry to execution layers (Vehicle, Teleoperation). This placement maximizes cohesion (all inference and baseline logic co-located) and minimizes diffusion (clean interfaces, no algorithmic leakage into execution or control layers).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not yet established as a distinct planning subsystem, the inference engine and baseline archive may be scattered across Vehicle and Teleoperation, creating maintenance and traceability debt.

Risk: If Tasking interface does not carry sufficient mission context (e.g., environmental constraints, priority weights), the inference engine may lack inputs needed to derive meaningful targets, forcing ad-hoc logic into Vehicle or Teleoperation.
Risk: If baseline archive grows without retention policy or indexing, historical auditability may degrade; recommend explicit data governance within NGHE Solution.

Risk: If SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (only inbound), safety-critical performance thresholds derived by the inference engine may not propagate back to Safety Services, creating a blind spot.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. Acceptance criteria explicitly require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baseline history. The NGHE Solution IM boundary directly supports both: (1) rule-based inference engine produces traceable targets from Tasking inputs; (2) hierarchical baseline archive maintains calibration history. Verification can be performed via audit of decision matrix outputs, baseline snapshots, and traceability logs. Interface contracts (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert bidirectional) are well-defined and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. FR-NGHE-005 (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context) maps unambiguously to NGHE Solution IM boundary. The IM scope explicitly includes mission planning, rule-based inference, decision matrix, and baseline archive—all necessary and sufficient to satisfy the FR. No ambiguity regarding ownership or responsibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 (combined) directly and completely address FR-NGHE-005. DP-NGHE-005.2 (rule-based inference engine + documented decision matrix with traceability) provides the derivation mechanism. DP-NGHE-005.3 (hierarchical baseline management + calibration history) provides the auditability mechanism. Together, they satisfy both the functional requirement (derive targets from mission context) and the acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, historical). No logical gaps or redundancy detected.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: SafetyAlert interface is marked bidirectional but containment analysis notes risk of unidirectional flow. If safety-critical performance thresholds derived by the inference engine do not propagate back to Safety Services, safety constraints may not be enforced during execution. Recommend explicit protocol for threshold feedback via SafetyAlert.

HIGH: Tasking interface must carry sufficient mission context (environmental constraints, priority weights, safety limits) to enable meaningful inference. If Tasking inputs are incomplete or ambiguous, the inference engine will lack grounding, forcing compensatory logic into Vehicle or Teleoperation. Recommend formal specification of Tasking message schema and validation rules.

MODERATE: Baseline archive retention and indexing policy is not explicitly assigned. Without governance, historical data may become orphaned or inaccessible, degrading auditability over time. Recommend explicit data lifecycle policy within NGHE Solution scope.

MODERATE: No mention of inference engine versioning or decision matrix change control. If rule updates are not tracked, traceability between performance targets and decision logic may be lost. Recommend version control and change log as part of baseline archive.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT. Containment score of 0.92 for NGHE Solution is well-justified. The rule-based inference engine and hierarchical baseline management are inherently mission-planning and performance-analysis functions with no natural home in Vehicle (execution), Teleoperation (operator interface), or Safety Services (safety-specific). Co-location in NGHE Solution maximizes cohesion: all inference logic, decision matrices, and baseline snapshots are centralized, enabling unified traceability and maintenance. Diffusion score of 0.08 reflects only necessary outbound interfaces (Telemetry) and inbound constraints (Tasking, SafetyAlert), with no algorithmic leakage into execution or control layers. This is a clean architectural boundary.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (SafetyAlert Protocol): Formalize the bidirectional SafetyAlert interface with explicit message types: (a) inbound: safety constraints that influence performance target derivation (e.g., max acceleration, no-go zones); (b) outbound: safety-critical performance thresholds derived by the inference engine (e.g., minimum clearance, abort conditions). Add acknowledgment handshake to ensure Safety Services receives and validates thresholds before execution.

REVISION 2 (Tasking Schema): Define a formal schema for Tasking inputs that specifies required fields: worksite mission objectives, environmental constraints (terrain, weather, visibility), operational priorities (speed vs. precision), safety limits, and calibration baseline reference. Include validation rules to reject incomplete or inconsistent inputs, preventing inference engine from operating on insufficient context.

REVISION 3 (Baseline Archive Governance): Establish explicit data lifecycle policy within NGHE Solution: (a) retention period (e.g., 12 months of snapshots); (b) indexing strategy (by mission ID, timestamp, baseline version); (c) archival mechanism (e.g., immutable log with cryptographic integrity); (d) access control (read-only for audit, write-only for inference engine). Document in NGHE Solution design specification.

REVISION 4 (Decision Matrix Versioning): Introduce version control for the rule-based inference engine and decision matrix. Each performance target output must include: (a) decision matrix version ID; (b) rule set version; (c) timestamp of derivation. Maintain a change log linking matrix updates to mission context changes or calibration events. This ensures full traceability from target to decision logic.

REVISION 5 (Telemetry Output Specification): Clarify Telemetry interface contract: performance targets and baseline snapshots must include metadata (derivation timestamp, decision matrix version, input parameters, confidence/uncertainty bounds). This enables Vehicle and Teleoperation to validate targets against current mission context and detect stale or inconsistent baselines.

REVISION 6 (Acceptance Criteria Operationalization): Map acceptance criteria to measurable IM-level tests: (a) Traceability: for each performance target, verify that a corresponding entry exists in the decision matrix with input parameters and rule chain documented; (b) History: verify that baseline archive contains timestamped snapshots with calibration events and can be queried by mission ID and date range; (c) Documentation: verify that decision matrix is human-readable and includes rationale for each rule.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR-NGHE-005 passes all three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) with strong logical coherence and excellent architectural containment (0.92). However, six critical and high-priority diffusion risks and interface specification gaps have been identified that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These risks directly impact safety criticality (high) and verification feasibility. Specifically: (1) SafetyAlert bidirectional protocol is underspecified, creating risk of safety-critical thresholds not propagating to Safety Services; (2) Tasking schema lacks formal specification, risking incomplete mission context to inference engine; (3) Baseline archive governance, decision matrix versioning, and telemetry metadata are not explicitly assigned to IM scope. Decomposing further without resolving these would propagate ambiguity and verification gaps into sub-levels. Revise DP and IM specifications to incorporate recommended revisions 1–6, then re-gate before deriving sub-FRs.

revise.why: DP-NGHE-005.2 and DP-NGHE-005.3 are logically sound but under-specified in interface contracts and governance. Revisions required: (1) Formalize SafetyAlert bidirectional protocol with explicit message types (inbound safety constraints, outbound safety-critical thresholds) and acknowledgment handshake (REVISION 1); (2) Define formal Tasking schema with required fields (mission objectives, environmental constraints, operational priorities, safety limits, baseline reference) and validation rules (REVISION 2); (3) Establish explicit baseline archive data lifecycle policy within NGHE Solution IM scope: retention period, indexing strategy, archival mechanism, access control (REVISION 3); (4) Introduce version control for inference engine and decision matrix with metadata (version ID, rule set version, derivation timestamp) and change log (REVISION 4); (5) Clarify Telemetry output specification to include derivation metadata, decision matrix version, input parameters, and confidence bounds (REVISION 5); (6) Operationalize acceptance criteria as measurable IM-level tests: traceability audit, history query, documentation review (REVISION 6). These revisions must be incorporated into the NGHE Solution design specification and re-validated against the DP-IM gate before sub-FR decomposition proceeds.

assumptions: SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional and capable of carrying both inbound safety constraints and outbound performance thresholds with acknowledgment semantics.
Tasking inputs will be formally specified and validated before reaching the inference engine; incomplete or ambiguous inputs will be rejected.
Baseline archive will be governed by explicit data lifecycle policy (retention, indexing, archival, access control) within NGHE Solution scope.
Decision matrix and rule-based inference engine will be version-controlled with full change history and traceability metadata.
Telemetry outputs will include derivation metadata (timestamp, decision matrix version, input parameters, confidence bounds) to enable Vehicle and Teleoperation validation.
Acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, historical) will be operationalized as measurable IM-level tests (traceability audit, history query, documentation review).
Safety criticality (high) requires that safety-critical performance thresholds derived by the inference engine must propagate to Safety Services with explicit acknowledgment before execution.
open_risks: CRITICAL: SafetyAlert bidirectional flow may be unidirectional in practice. If safety-critical performance thresholds do not propagate back to Safety Services, safety constraints may not be enforced during execution. Mitigation: formalize protocol with explicit message types and acknowledgment handshake.

HIGH: Tasking interface may carry insufficient mission context (environmental constraints, priority weights, safety limits). If inputs are incomplete or ambiguous, inference engine will lack grounding, forcing compensatory logic into Vehicle or Teleoperation. Mitigation: define formal Tasking schema with validation rules.

MODERATE: Baseline archive retention and indexing policy is not explicitly assigned. Without governance, historical data may become orphaned or inaccessible, degrading auditability over time. Mitigation: establish explicit data lifecycle policy within NGHE Solution scope.

MODERATE: No mention of inference engine versioning or decision matrix change control. If rule updates are not tracked, traceability between performance targets and decision logic may be lost. Mitigation: introduce version control and change log as part of baseline archive.

MODERATE: Telemetry output specification does not include derivation metadata. Vehicle and Teleoperation may not be able to validate targets against current mission context or detect stale/inconsistent baselines. Mitigation: clarify Telemetry interface contract with metadata requirements.

MODERATE: Acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, historical) are stated functionally but not operationalized as measurable IM-level tests. Verification may be subjective or incomplete. Mitigation: map acceptance criteria to explicit audit procedures and test cases.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of SafetyAlert bidirectional protocol: message types (inbound safety constraints, outbound safety-critical thresholds), data formats, acknowledgment handshake, timeout/retry semantics.

Formal schema for Tasking inputs: required fields (mission objectives, environmental constraints, operational priorities, safety limits, baseline reference), data types, validation rules, error handling.

Baseline archive data lifecycle policy: retention period, indexing strategy (by mission ID, timestamp, baseline version), archival mechanism (immutable log, cryptographic integrity), access control (read-only for audit, write-only for inference engine).
Version control and change log for inference engine and decision matrix: version ID scheme, rule set versioning, change log format, traceability to mission context changes or calibration events.
Telemetry output specification: metadata requirements (derivation timestamp, decision matrix version, input parameters, confidence/uncertainty bounds), data formats, validation rules.

Operationalized acceptance criteria tests: (a) Traceability audit procedure (verify decision matrix entry for each performance target with input parameters and rule chain); (b) History query procedure (verify baseline archive contains timestamped snapshots queryable by mission ID and date range); (c) Documentation review procedure (verify decision matrix is human-readable with rationale for each rule).
Safety Services interface contract: explicit protocol for receiving and validating safety-critical performance thresholds from NGHE Solution, acknowledgment mechanism, enforcement timeline.
